items.front: What year did the U.S. and Canada establish their cross-border ATC procedures agreement?
1985 agreement between U.S. and Canada allowing use of each other's ATC procedures in the other's airspace, with exceptions for host-country laws and unique operational requirements.
items.back: Why Chapter 12 was revised to include only Canadian procedures required by Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirements.

items.front: What limits one country from using its own ATC procedures in the other country's airspace under the 1985 agreement?
items.back: The procedures must not be inconsistent with, or repugnant to, the laws and regulations or unique operational requirements of the country over whose territory the airspace is located.

items.front: Canadian Class A: vertical limits
items.back: Base of all controlled high level airspace up to and including FL 600. Only IFR flights permitted.

items.front: Canadian Class B: vertical limits
items.back: Above 12,500 feet ASL or at/above MEA (whichever higher) up to but NOT including 18,000 feet ASL. Only IFR and CVFR flights permitted.

items.front: Base of all controlled high level airspace up to and including FL 600. Only IFR flights permitted.
items.back: Canadian Class A airspace.

items.front: Above 12,500 feet ASL or MEA (whichever higher) up to but not including 18,000 feet ASL. Only IFR and CVFR flights. IFR procedures applied to CVFR.
items.back: Canadian Class B airspace.

items.front: Canadian VFR entry requirements: Class C vs Class D vs Class E
items.back: Class C: ATC clearance required for VFR entry. Class D: two-way communications required before entry (no clearance). Class E: no special VFR requirements.

items.front: What are the two sub-types of Canadian Class F airspace?
items.back: Class F advisory and Class F restricted.

items.front: Uncontrolled airspace where ATC has neither the authority nor responsibility for exercising control over air traffic.
items.back: Canadian Class G airspace.

items.front: What is the CVFR pilot responsible for at all times?
items.back: Maintaining VFR flight and visual reference to the ground at all times.

items.front: What is the Canadian substitute clearance for VFR-on-top, and what are its two requirements?
items.back: "At least 1,000 feet-on-top" in lieu of VFR-on-top. Requirements: (a) pilot requests it; (b) pilot will not operate in Class A or Class B airspace.

items.front: Pilot must ensure at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with 3 miles or more flight visibility. Pilot's request = confirmation conditions are adequate.
items.back: Pilot's weather responsibility for the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance in Canada.

items.front: Who is responsible for verifying weather conditions for the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance?
items.back: The pilot. It is the pilot's responsibility to ensure the operation can be conducted at least 1,000 feet above all cloud/haze/smoke with 3 miles flight visibility. The pilot's request is considered confirmation that conditions are adequate.

items.front: Canadian separation rule for IFR and CVFR aircraft
items.back: Apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum between aircraft operating in accordance with an IFR or CVFR clearance, regardless of the weather conditions.

items.front: Does good weather eliminate the separation requirement between IFR and CVFR aircraft in Canadian airspace?
items.back: No. The separation minimum applies regardless of the weather conditions. CVFR aircraft receive IFR-equivalent separation treatment.

items.front: Canadian radio-failure altitude rule for turbine aircraft cleared short of destination
items.back: Maintain last assigned altitude/flight level or MEA (whichever higher) until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at filed altitude(s)/flight level(s). Does NOT apply if ATC issued alternate radio-failure instructions.

items.front: When does the border-crossing climb commence for a turbine aircraft that will enter U.S. airspace within 10 min of the clearance limit?
items.back: At the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary — NOT immediately after the clearance limit, NOT 10 min after, and NOT after entering U.S. airspace.

items.front: Which aircraft types are subject to the Canadian radio-failure 10-minute-beyond-clearance-limit rule?
items.back: Turbine-powered aircraft: turboprop or turbojet.

items.front: What authorization is required before ATC may approve parachute jumping in Canadian airspace?
items.back: Prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority. Canadian regulations require written authority from the Ministry of Transport.

items.front: Written authority from the Ministry of Transport is required before ATC may authorize parachute jumping.
items.back: Canadian parachute jumping rule, per 12-1-6 and its note.

items.front: Canadian SVFR approval criteria: controller on duty vs. no controller on duty
items.back: Controller ON duty: IFR traffic ONLY. No controller: BOTH IFR traffic AND official ceiling/visibility reports.

items.front: Canadian SVFR weather minima: aircraft (non-helicopter) vs. helicopter
items.back: Aircraft: 1 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when reported). Helicopters: 1/2 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when available).

items.front: Do pilots need IFR qualification for night SVFR in Canada?
items.back: No. Pilots do not have to be IFR qualified to fly SVFR at night in Canada, nor does the aircraft have to be equipped for IFR flight. This differs from U.S. rules.

items.front: 1/2 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when available) for SVFR.
items.back: Canadian SVFR weather minimum for helicopters.

items.front: 1 mile flight visibility (ground visibility when reported) for SVFR.
items.back: Canadian SVFR weather minimum for aircraft other than helicopters.
